perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall not, without the prior written permission of InterTrust's Chairman or such other InterTrust Officer as who has been designated in writing by InterTrust's Chairman (a "Designated InterTrust --------------------- Officer"), disclose Confidential Information to any person other than as set ------- forth immediately above.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall endeavor not to, without what they perceive as appropriate written permission of InterTrust's Chairman or such other InterTrust Officer as who has been designated in writing by InterTrust's Chairman (a "Designated InterTrust --------------------- Officer"), disclose Confidential Information to any person other than as set ------- forth immediately above.
perturbation.explanation: The added phrase introduces ambiguity. 'Endeavor' suggests an attempt rather than a strict obligation, and 'what they perceive as appropriate' makes the required permission subjective, creating uncertainty about what constitutes valid authorization.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially violating the Trade Secrets Act, which aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. It protects information from being disclosed in an improper manner, but this now suggests that an employee could disclose in a manner they see fit.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text states "Recipient shall not... disclose Confidential Information", imposing a strict prohibition. The changed text replaces this with "Recipient shall endeavor not to... disclose Confidential Information", which weakens the obligation to merely an attempt. Further, the clause "without what they perceive as appropriate written permission" introduces a subjective element, potentially allowing disclosure based on the recipient's perception rather than a clear authorization. This contradicts the intent of protecting trade secrets, as the unauthorized disclosure could be defended as being perceived as appropriate by the recipient. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to civil actions against the misappropriation of trade secrets, and although the snippet is not directly applicable, the Law Explanation explains that the modification suggests an employee could disclose in a manner they see fit.

perturbation.original_text: The undersigned will not use Confidential Information except in fulfillment of the undersigned employee's and/or other individual's obligations with NatWest, and for no other purposes whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned will attempt not to use Confidential Information except in general alignment with the undersigned employee's and/or other individual's obligations with NatWest, and for no other purposes deemed severely inappropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the obligation. 'Attempt not to use' suggests less than a strict prohibition, 'general alignment' makes the scope of permissible use vague, and 'deemed severely inappropriate' introduces a subjective standard.
perturbation.law_explanation: This weakens the obligation to only use information for employment duties. CUTSA is intended to prevent even unintentional misuse of trade secrets, which this modification does not.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating a contradiction. The original text states the information will not be used 'except in fulfillment of...obligations with NatWest, and for no other purposes whatsoever'. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'attempt not to use Confidential Information except in general alignment with...obligations with NatWest, and for no other purposes deemed severely inappropriate'. The law explanation notes that CUTSA is intended to prevent even unintentional misuse of trade secrets, while the modified text weakens the original obligation.

perturbation.original_text: The undersigned will return all physical embodiments of Confidential Information in the undersigned's possession to InterTrust promptly upon request by InterTrust, and in no event later than fifteen (15) days thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned will endeavor to return most physical embodiments of Confidential Information located in the undersigned's primary possession to InterTrust within a reasonable timeframe upon request by InterTrust.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will return all' to 'will endeavor to return most' weakens the obligation. 'Primary possession' narrows the scope. 'Reasonable timeframe' replaces a definite deadline, creating ambiguity and subjectivity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The act addresses the theft of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to return confidential information, it makes it more ambiguous if failure to return would be a violation. Making it less certain whether the secret will be kept safe and confidential.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The original contract language, 'will return all physical embodiments of Confidential Information... in no event later than fifteen (15) days thereafter,' establishes a clear and enforceable obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation by using 'will endeavor to return most,' which is subjective and less enforceable. Moreover, replacing the definite 15-day deadline with 'reasonable timeframe' introduces ambiguity. According to the LLM Law Explanation, this weakening makes it 'more ambiguous if failure to return would be a violation,' which undermines the protection of trade secrets, potentially violating 18 USC 1832 concerning the theft of trade secrets, as the certainty of keeping the secret safe is reduced. The change introduces Ambiguity in place of a previously concrete requirement to return property which undermines the original obligation.

perturbation.original_text: In addition, the Recipient shall be entitled to disclose Confidential Information pursuant to a court order issued by a court of competent jurisdiction or as otherwise required by law; provided that the undersigned -------- ---- shall provide prompt advance notice thereof to InterTrust to enable InterTrust to seek a protective order or otherwise prevent such disclosure and shall disclose no more than the minimum information required by such court order or by law.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, the Recipient may feel entitled to disclose Confidential Information pursuant to what appears to be a court order or legal requirement; provided that the undersigned -------- ---- shall attempt to provide some notice to InterTrust if reasonably practical, and shall disclose only information they believe is needed.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces several layers of ambiguity. 'May feel entitled' replaces a firm entitlement with a subjective belief. 'What appears to be' introduces uncertainty about the validity of the legal requirement. 'Attempt to provide some notice if reasonably practical' weakens the notice requirement, and 'information they believe is needed' replaces the objective standard of 'minimum information required' with a subjective belief.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 26(c) allows parties to seek protective orders to prevent the disclosure of confidential information. The original text requires prompt notice to allow InterTrust to seek such an order and limits disclosure to the minimum required. The revised text weakens this significantly, by not giving InterTrust enough time to ensure the information is protected. It also gives the power to the party disclosing the information to decide how much to disclose, when the ruling states minimum. In any instance, confidential information disclosed without good reason is a breach of trust.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities that weaken the original obligations, potentially hindering InterTrust's ability to protect its confidential information. The original text mandates 'prompt advance notice' to enable InterTrust to seek a protective order, while the changed text only requires an 'attempt to provide some notice if reasonably practical'. This weakens InterTrust's ability to respond to potential disclosures. The original text limits disclosure to the 'minimum information required,' but the revised text allows disclosure of 'information they believe is needed,' introducing subjectivity and potentially allowing for broader disclosure than necessary. This contradicts the purpose of FRCP 26(c), as interpreted by the LLM, which seeks to prevent the disclosure of confidential information, by weakening the mechanisms designed to ensure information protection.
